content.text: Architecture drift occurs when a codebase gradually diverges from its intended design without any single change being large enough to trigger a review. In GenAI systems, drift is particularly dangerous because the affected properties — adapter isolation, prompt governance, observability contracts, and safety configurations — are the ones that prevent production incidents, not the ones that cause visible feature regressions.

This document defines a five-stage drift detection lifecycle for GenAI architectures: capturing a baseline snapshot, running scheduled scans, classifying drift findings by severity, routing alerts, and managing the remediation and re-baselining workflow.

attrs.alt: Architecture drift detection lifecycle: baseline → scan → classify → alert → remediate

content.text: What Architecture Drift Looks Like in GenAI Systems
Drift accumulates through small, individually reasonable decisions that compound over time:

A developer adds a quick fix that calls the Anthropic SDK directly from a service class, bypassing the adapter. Six months later, there are four direct imports scattered across three services, and the adapter is no longer the single point of control.
After the safety baseline was introduced, two prompt templates that predated the policy were never updated. They still pass CI because the lint check only runs on changed files in PRs.
The OTel span schema was updated to require
as a required attribute. Three of eight LLM-calling services never added it, so cost attribution by feature is broken for 40% of traffic.
A developer updated the model field in a prompt YAML to test a new model in production and forgot to revert it. The change has been running for three weeks with no alert.
Traffic grew 50% over a quarter. The cost alerts were set against the old baseline and never updated. The team has been over-budget for two months without an alert firing.

Fitness functions in CI catch violations when they are introduced. Drift detection catches violations that existed before the fitness function was written, or that entered through paths CI does not scan — direct commits, emergency hotfixes, infrastructure changes.
The baseline is a snapshot of the codebase's architectural state at a known-good point in time. It is stored as a set of structured artefacts in
and committed to the repository. When the baseline is updated, the update must go through a PR and requires tech lead approval.
For each module, which other modules it imports; which external packages it uses
For each prompt YAML: id, version, model, max_tokens, safety_baseline_version, injection_surface
Required OTel span attributes as defined in the observability contract
Per-feature: p50/p95 cost per request, monthly total, as of baseline date
Public methods of each adapter class with parameter signatures

# scripts/capture_baseline.py
# Run after a major architectural change is approved and merged.
# Creates/updates .arch-baseline/ artefacts.
# Must be reviewed and merged via PR with tech lead approval.

import ast, json, pathlib, yaml

def build_import_graph() -> dict:
    graph = {}
    for path in pathlib.Path("src/").rglob("*.py"):
        try:
            tree = ast.parse(path.read_text())
        except SyntaxError:
            continue
        imports = set()
        for node in ast.walk(tree):
            if isinstance(node, ast.Import):
                imports.update(alias.name.split(".")[0] for alias in node.names)
            elif isinstance(node, ast.ImportFrom) and node.module:
                imports.add(node.module.split(".")[0])
        graph[str(path)] = sorted(imports)
    return graph

def build_prompt_inventory() -> list:
    import re
    inventory = []
    for path in pathlib.Path("prompts/").glob("**/*.yaml"):
        data = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
        m = re.search(r"safety baseline v(\d+\.\d+)", data.get("system", ""), re.I)
        inventory.append({
            "id":                data.get("id", path.stem),
            "path":              str(path),
            "version":           data.get("version"),
            "model":             data.get("model"),
            "max_tokens":        data.get("max_tokens"),
            "safety_baseline":   m.group(1) if m else None,
            "injection_surface": data.get("injection_surface", "none"),
        })
    return inventory

baseline_dir = pathlib.Path(".arch-baseline/")
baseline_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

(baseline_dir / "import_graph.json").write_text(json.dumps(build_import_graph(), indent=2))
(baseline_dir / "prompt_inventory.json").write_text(json.dumps(build_prompt_inventory(), indent=2))
print("Baseline captured in .arch-baseline/")
The drift scan runs daily on the default branch and on demand. It compares the current state of the codebase against the stored baseline and produces a structured list of findings.
Each finding is classified by severity and category. Severity determines the alert channel and SLA for resolution. Category determines who owns remediation.
Direct SDK import outside adapter, business logic calling LLM directly
Safety baseline missing, version regressed, new prompt not in baseline
Required OTel attribute missing from span, cost metric not emitted
Cost up >50% vs baseline with no traffic increase, max_tokens regressed
Model changed without version bump or budget update
Block next production deployment if unresolved
Block next non-hotfix deployment if unresolved >3 days
Weekly drift digest email + GitHub issue
No deployment block; tracked in backlog

When a drift finding is resolved, the team has three options: fix the code to comply with the intended architecture, accept the drift and update the baseline to reflect an intentional change, or defer it with a ticket.
Direct SDK import in service class
Safety baseline below minimum in old prompt
New prompt not in baseline inventory
Model changed to more expensive version
If intentional: update baseline + cost docs
Cost 50% above baseline due to traffic growth
Update baseline to reflect new traffic
Never — cost must be tracked accurately
Create a PR modifying the relevant artefact in
PR description must explain why the baseline is changing (intentional architectural decision, traffic growth, new feature)
Tech lead reviews and approves the PR
After merge, the drift scan no longer flags the accepted change
If the baseline update covers a CRITICAL finding, it must be accompanied by a corresponding code fix — not just a baseline update that hides the violation

# .github/workflows/arch-drift-scan.yml
name: Architecture Drift Scan

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'    # Daily at 06:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  drift-scan:
    name: "Architecture Drift Detection"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install pyyaml requests

      - name: Run drift scan
        run: python scripts/scan_architecture_drift.py > drift_findings.json

      - name: Route findings
        run: python scripts/route_drift_findings.py < drift_findings.json
        env:
          SLACK_CRITICAL_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_CRITICAL_WEBHOOK }}
          SLACK_ARCH_ALERTS_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_ARCH_ALERTS_WEBHOOK }}
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          GITHUB_REPOSITORY: ${{ github.repository }}

      - name: Upload findings artefact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: drift-findings-${{ github.run_number }}
          path: drift_findings.json

      - name: Block on CRITICAL findings
        run: |
          CRITICAL=$(python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('drift_findings.json')); print(sum(1 for f in d['findings'] if f['severity']=='CRITICAL'))")
          [ "$CRITICAL" -eq "0" ] || { echo "::error::$CRITICAL CRITICAL drift finding(s) — deploy blocked"; exit 1; }
Each CRITICAL requires a post-mortem on how it entered without CI catching it
Escalate to EM; improve the CI gate that should have caught it
If backlog of HIGH findings grows, schedule an architecture fitness sprint
High frequency suggests architectural instability — revisit design
Tighten scan logic; false positives erode trust in the process
Deferred findings must either be fixed or formally accepted into baseline
Integrating Drift Detection into the Development Workflow
Add a checkbox: 'If this PR changes an architectural property, I have run
and submitted a baseline update PR.'
Add 'Did architecture drift contribute to this incident?' to the post-mortem template. If yes, identify which drift check would have caught it earlier and improve it.
Review open arch-drift GitHub issues at sprint planning. CRITICAL and HIGH findings are treated as bugs — they go into the sprint, not the backlog.
Before each quarterly architecture review, run the drift scan manually and include the findings in the agenda. Patterns of repeated drift in the same category indicate a process gap.
New team members read the baseline artefacts in
as part of onboarding. The baseline is a readable description of the intended architecture — not just a technical snapshot.
Architecture drift in GenAI systems accumulates through small individually reasonable decisions: direct SDK imports, unenforced safety baselines, missing OTel attributes, and unreviewed model changes
A five-stage drift detection lifecycle catches drift that CI fitness functions miss: capture a baseline → daily scan → classify findings → route alerts → remediate and re-baseline
CRITICAL findings (adapter bypass, missing safety baseline) must be resolved the same day and block production deployments if outstanding
The baseline is a versioned, reviewed artefact stored in the repository — updating it requires a PR with tech lead approval, preventing silent architectural resets

Drift detection supplements, not replaces, CI fitness functions: fitness functions catch violations at PR time; drift detection catches violations that entered before the fitness function was written or through paths CI does not scan
Track CRITICAL findings per month as the primary architecture health metric; any non-zero month requires a post-mortem on the gap in CI coverage
